text.title: Build-in-Public Engine Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that keep a build-in-public motion compounding rather than burning out at week 6.

rule.statement: Every week, the founder fills the weekly-input-log with four fields: what I shipped, what broke, what I learned, one number. Posts MUST be derived from this log. Posting without writing the log first is forbidden — it produces opinion-spam, not build-in-public content.

rationale.source: James Clear, Atomic Habits + Pieter Levels public archive

rule.rationale: Build-in-public dies when posts become hot takes. Tethering every post to actual product work keeps the content concrete, ICP-anchored, and inexhaustible (the work is the input).

rule.statement: Use exactly five standard formats: (1) build-log post, (2) learning post, (3) screenshot/demo, (4) number-of-the-week, (5) ask post. Free-form ranting, news commentary, or general "thought leader" takes are NOT part of the engine. The weekly cadence is roughly 3 short (formats 1-4) + 1 longer thread (format 1 or 2 expanded).

rationale.source: Empirical analysis of high-growth indie accounts 2022-2025

rule.rationale: Constrained format menus outperform open posting because (a) the founder spends less time deciding what to post, (b) the audience learns to recognize the patterns, (c) the engine survives low-creativity weeks.

rule.statement: Every post starts with a hook line: a number, a contradiction, a question, or a screenshot. Hooks that begin "I think...", "Here's my take...", or "Random thought:" are rejected. The hook is testable: if the first line were the only thing the reader saw, would they stop scrolling?

rationale.source: X / Twitter engagement data + Wes Kao writing courses

rule.rationale: First-line dwell time is the dominant signal in algorithmic feeds. Generic openers train the algorithm and the audience to skip. Hook discipline is the single highest-leverage habit in the engine.

rule.statement: Long-form threads (format 1 or 2 expanded) MUST be repurposed within 7 days to: LinkedIn post, blog/Substack article, newsletter snippet. The repurpose is not a copy-paste — it's a re-edit for the platform's reading mode and a CTA that points back to the email list. Threads that never leave X waste 70% of their reach potential.

rationale.source: Justin Welsh content operating system (5M+ revenue from one repurposing chain)

rule.rationale: Each repurpose hits a non-overlapping audience and converts a fraction to email — the only owned channel. X account loss / algorithm shifts destroy non-repurposed engines.

rule.statement: Track ONLY: weekly net-new followers (or list subscribers), weekly inbound DMs/replies of substance (signal not vanity), and monthly newsletter signups. Reject likes, impressions, and view counts as primary metrics — they are vanity in the build-in-public context. Monthly review uses these to decide what to keep, drop, and amplify.
rule.rationale: Vanity metrics rise even when the engine is failing. Compound, owned-channel metrics (list, substantive DMs) correlate with actual business outcomes. Founders who measure likes optimize for a metric that doesn't pay them.

example.title: Build-log post with hook
example.code:
shipped today: refunds now process in 12s instead of 4min.
three weeks of work. fixed by moving one query out of a Python loop.
screenshot of before/after timing in the thread.
example.note: Number-led hook, concrete what-shipped, contradiction (3 weeks for "one query"), screenshot promised. Format 1 + 3 combined.

example.title: Generic LLM-generated post
example.code: Building in public is hard but rewarding. Here are 5 lessons every founder should know about consistency... 1/ Show up every day 2/ Be authentic 3/ ...
example.note: No work tethered, no specifics, no hook discipline. Fails r1, r3. This is the dominant LLM failure mode.
